title: The disciples argue part 1
scripture_text: ‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?” He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them and he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea. Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’
devotional:
Mark 9v33-37.

‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said ‘’Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.’’ He took a little child whom he placed among them. Taking the child in his arms, he said to them, ‘’Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.”

The question Jesus asked

There are a number of lessons we can learn from how Jesus dealt with the disciples at this time. In order to address the problem Jesus asked the question. The word Mark uses for ask is a very strong word eperotao which means (a) an especially earnest enquiry, (b) an insistent pressing interrogation, or (c) an enquiry with a certain legal formality about it. This was more than an ‘off the cuff’ question, but a serious interrogation. Jesus was demanding an answer from them.

Sometimes we need to push people in order to get out what is really in their hearts. Jesus knew that this was such an important issue and if he didn't bring it to the surface then the disciples would not be able to do the ministry they had been called to.

Jesus waited until they were 'in the house' before he began to address the problem. He didn't do it in public, but waited until he was alone with them. It was in 'the house' a place where they felt secure and at home. Jesus was very direct in his questioning 'What were you arguing about?' This was more than a friendly chat. Dialogizomai the word for arguing, is from a word that can refer to simple conversation or to an argument and dispute among parties. Here all the disciples were party to a dispute. The imperfect tense tells us that they kept on arguing. Over and over again they kept picking at each other. We have recorded three different occasions when the disciples argue about which of them would be the greatest --here in Mark 9:46-48, just before Jesus' final ministry in Jerusalem (Matthew 20:20-28), and, amazingly, at the Last Supper (Luke 22:24). The disciples had an ongoing problem with who was the greatest, so Jesus needed to address this issue.

The response of the disciples

Mark tells us 'But they kept quiet.' This response reminds me of a typical teenager, when asked a question simply grunts, or says 'l don't know.' The question Jesus asked made them feel uncomfortable, so instead of being honest and open, they remained silent. The disciples understand the inappropriateness of their conversation. While Jesus was telling them to expect his betrayal and death, they were thinking about their place in the kingdom.

This was a moment of high drama as an awkward silence descended upon the room. I can just imagine the disciples looking at each other, as Jesus patiently waits for an answer. The word Mark uses for 'kept quiet' is the Greek word siopao, which carries the idea of involuntary silence. I think this is important, while the disciples were arguing as they were going along the road they had some sense of control over where the debate was going, they could steer it, each one could manipulate it to their own advantage. But now Jesus had entered the debate, not as a contributor but as the one who would take control, revealing the real issues. This in turn means that the disciples had no more control over where the debate was going, so in their vulnerability they retreat into silence, no longer wishing to express their opinion. The fact that the silence was involuntary, indicates that the disciples were no longer in charge.
prayer: Father forgive me for the times I have given you the silent treatment.
html:
The disciples argue part 1

Study Text: Matthew 18v1-9. Mark 9v33-37
‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?” He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them and he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea. Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’

Mark 9v33-37.

‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said ‘’Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.’’ He took a little child whom he placed among them. Taking the child in his arms, he said to them, ‘’Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.”

There are a number of lessons we can learn from how Jesus dealt with the disciples at this time. In order to address the problem Jesus asked the question. The word Mark uses for ask is a very strong word eperotao which means (a) an especially earnest enquiry, (b) an insistent pressing interrogation, or (c) an enquiry with a certain legal formality about it. This was more than an ‘off the cuff’ question, but a serious interrogation. Jesus was demanding an answer from them.

Sometimes we need to push people in order to get out what is really in their hearts. Jesus knew that this was such an important issue and if he didn't bring it to the surface then the disciples would not be able to do the ministry they had been called to.

It was in 'the house' a place where they felt secure and at home. Jesus was very direct in his questioning 'What were you arguing about?' This was more than a friendly chat. We have recorded three different occasions when the disciples argue about which of them would be the greatest --here in Mark 9:46-48, just before Jesus' final ministry in Jerusalem (Matthew 20:20-28), and, amazingly, at the Last Supper (Luke 22:24). The disciples had an ongoing problem with who was the greatest, so Jesus needed to address this issue.

Mark tells us 'But they kept quiet.' This response reminds me of a typical teenager, when asked a question simply grunts, or says 'l don't know.' The question Jesus asked made them feel uncomfortable, so instead of being honest and open, they remained silent. The disciples understand the inappropriateness of their conversation. While Jesus was telling them to expect his betrayal and death, they were thinking about their place in the kingdom.

This was a moment of high drama as an awkward silence descended upon the room. I can just imagine the disciples looking at each other, as Jesus patiently waits for an answer. The word Mark uses for 'kept quiet' is the Greek word siopao, which carries the idea of involuntary silence. I think this is important, while the disciples were arguing as they were going along the road they had some sense of control over where the debate was going, they could steer it, each one could manipulate it to their own advantage. But now Jesus had entered the debate, not as a contributor but as the one who would take control, revealing the real issues. This in turn means that the disciples had no more control over where the debate was going, so in their vulnerability they retreat into silence, no longer wishing to express their opinion. The fact that the silence was involuntary, indicates that the disciples were no longer in charge.

Prayer: Father forgive me for the times I have given you the silent treatment.
Bible in a year reading plan: - 1 Samuel 23:1-25:31
